3 min read
Just now

--

This is actually nice. CS50 Lecture 1 looks like a standard “intro to C” class until you realize Malan spends the last 15 minutes running through production bugs that killed planes, broke arcade machines, and made your bank math wrong.
Three bugs, one root cause.
Integer Overflow — The 2 Billion Ceiling
A standard int in C uses 32 bits. With signed integers you get roughly 2 billion on each side. Sounds like a lot until you’re counting tenths of a second inside a plane that stays powered on for 248 days straight.
That’s exactly what happened with the Boeing 787.
A software counter inside the Generator Control Unit was ticking in tenths of a second. After 248 days of continuous power it hit 2,147,483,647 — the max value for a signed 32-bit integer — and overflowed back to zero. The plane lost all AC electrical power mid-flight.
The FAA directive literally said: “This condition is caused by a software counter internal to the GCUs that will overflow after 248 days of continuous power.”
The temporary fix? Reboot the plane. Turn it off and back on. Same as your phone. The permanent fix was a software upgrade that probably just used a 64-bit counter.
Someone chose a 32-bit integer for a timer and never did the math on how long it would last.
Pacman’s Kill Screen
